title: 24 Tiqimt — The Arrival in Egypt & The Assembly
narrative:
Tiqimt

The Arrival of Christ in Egypt (Monthly Commemoration)

St. Arethas and the Martyrs of Najran (Monthly Commemoration)

St. Elesbaan (King Kaleb of Aksum)

The twenty-fourth day of Tiqimt carries one of the most historically specific and most Ethiopian of all the calendar's commemorations: the monthly feast of the Martyrs of Najran, and the feast of St. Elesbaan — King Kaleb of Aksum — whose military intervention on their behalf is one of the most dramatic moments in the history of the ancient Christian world. In the year 523 A.D., the Jewish king Yusuf Dhu Nuwas of the Himyarite kingdom in southern Arabia launched a systematic massacre of the Christian community of Najran — a prosperous oasis city in what is now northwestern Saudi Arabia, inhabited by a large and well-established Arab Christian community. Arethas — Al-Harith ibn Ka'b in Arabic — was the leading nobleman of Najran, and when Dhu Nuwas entered the city and demanded apostasy, Arethas led the community's refusal. The subsequent massacre was one of the worst single acts of anti-Christian violence in the ancient world: hundreds, perhaps thousands, were killed — burned alive, thrown into fire-trenches, executed by sword — including women, children, and clergy, and Arethas himself was among the first to die, an old man who had lived his whole life in the covenant of faith and who died in it without recantation.

The news of the Najran massacre reached King Kaleb of Aksum — Elesbaan in the Greek tradition — and his response was the decisive military and spiritual act that defines his place in history. He assembled the Aksumite fleet, consulted with the Patriarch of Alexandria, and led a military expedition across the Red Sea to Arabia. The campaign was successful: Dhu Nuwas was defeated, the Christian community of Najran was avenged, and a Christian king was installed in the Himyarite kingdom. Kaleb's campaign was not purely political; the sources describe it explicitly as a response to the persecution of Christians, as a military expression of the solidarity that the covenant community owes to those of its members who are being destroyed elsewhere. Kaleb understood himself to be acting as the arm of the covenant — bringing the Aksumite kingdom's power to bear in defense of the helpless, in the most direct possible interpretation of what the covenant of mercy demands from those who have the capacity to act.

What makes Kaleb's story still more extraordinary is its sequel. After his victory in Arabia, having achieved what no other Christian king had managed — the military rescue of a persecuted community at the cost of a major naval expedition — Kaleb returned to Aksum, removed his crown, and sent it to be hung in the Church of the Holy Sepulchre in Jerusalem. He then abdicated in favor of his son and entered a monastery, where he lived out his remaining years as a monk. The great king who had commanded fleets and armies chose the cell as his final vocation, understanding — as the tradition consistently teaches — that the exterior act of mercy, however great, is incomplete without the interior act of self-offering. His monastic name, Gebre Mesqel — the same name we encountered on the fourteenth day in his son — was the family's shared declaration: Servant of the Cross.

The Arrival in Egypt, the Martyrs of Najran, and King Kaleb together on this twenty-fourth day of Tiqimt compose the calendar's most complete single-day statement about the Covenant of Mercy in its Ethiopian dimension. The Holy Family's arrival in Egypt as refugees is the archetype of the divine solidarity with the vulnerable — God Himself choosing displacement and helplessness in order to be near those who suffer it. The Najran martyrs are the community that held the covenant of mercy in its most extreme test, refusing to surrender what they had received regardless of the cost. King Kaleb is the community member who had the power to respond and used it without hesitation — who crossed the Red Sea not for trade or territory but for the blood of his brothers in faith — and who then recognized that the truest form of his sovereignty was the monk's submission. The month of the Covenant of Mercy reaches its most historically precise and most deeply Ethiopian expression on this twenty-fourth day.
